institutions.institution_blurb: United States Military Academy (West Point), class of 1926 (B.S.).

cross_connections.display_label: Both Hooker descendants in American broadcasting — Munson founding Armed Forces Radio; Hulbert Taft publishing the Cincinnati Times-Star.

notable.notable_blurb: Founder of Armed Forces Radio Service

narrative_blocks.header: He founded Armed Forces Radio Service
narrative_blocks.body: As chief of the Army Information Branch from 1941, Munson created Armed Forces Radio, Yank Army Weekly, and the Army Pictorial Service — the voice, the paper, and the pictures of the GI war. Afterward he ran NBC’s film division and television operations.

narrative_blocks.header: Born on an Army post, died a general
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Fort Bayard to a brigadier general, West Point class of 1926, and a brigadier himself by 1945 — the Munsons were two generations of generals. He wrote Leadership for the American Soldier; ninety boxes of his papers rest at the University of Wyoming.

bio.display_name: Brig. Gen. Edward Lyman Munson Jr.
bio.title: Brigadier General

focus.n: Brig. Gen. Edward Lyman Munson Jr.

institutions.institution_blurb: United States Military Academy (West Point), class of 1926 (B.S.).

cross_connections.display_label: Both Hooker descendants in American broadcasting — Munson founding Armed Forces Radio; Hulbert Taft publishing the Cincinnati Times-Star.

notable.notable_blurb: Founder of Armed Forces Radio Service

narrative_blocks.header: He founded Armed Forces Radio Service
narrative_blocks.body: As chief of the Army Information Branch from 1941, Munson created Armed Forces Radio, Yank Army Weekly, and the Army Pictorial Service — the voice, the paper, and the pictures of the GI war. Afterward he ran NBC’s film division and television operations.

narrative_blocks.header: Born on an Army post, died a general
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Fort Bayard to a brigadier general, West Point class of 1926, and a brigadier himself by 1945 — the Munsons were two generations of generals. He wrote Leadership for the American Soldier; ninety boxes of his papers rest at the University of Wyoming.

bio.display_name: Brig. Gen. Edward Lyman Munson Jr.
bio.title: Brigadier General

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale School of Medicine, M.D. 1892; M.A. Yale 1893.

cross_connections.display_label: Served as health officer for Manila under Governor Taft in 1903 — two Hooker descendants, one running the Philippines.
Both were Bohemian Club members — two Hooker descendants in the same institution.

notable.notable_blurb: Designed the Munson Last G.I. shoe 1912

narrative_blocks.body: Munson spent four years studying the feet of 2,000 soldiers, then designed a shoe last — snug in heel and arch, with a roomy upturned toe. The Munson Last became U.S. military footwear from 1912 onward: the hobnail brogan of WWI and the G.I. shoe of WWII.

narrative_blocks.header: He attended McKinley after the shooting
narrative_blocks.body: Munson was one of the physicians who attended President McKinley in Buffalo after the assassination attempt on September 6, 1901. Two years later he served as health officer for Manila under Governor William Howard Taft — another Hooker descendant. He survived tuberculosis contracted in the Philippines, and in 1923 commanded American medical relief after the Great Kantō earthquake in Japan.

narrative_blocks.header: She is buried at Arlington National Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Jane Schneeloch Munson married Brigadier General Edward Lyman Munson in 1893 and survived him by seven years, dying in 1954 at age 82. They are buried together at Arlington National Cemetery. FindAGrave records her first name as 'Marta' — most likely a transcription of 'Martha'.

HD0570.prominent_name_reason: Lyman Ezra Munson — Yale Law School 1851; first judge appointed by President Lincoln to the Supreme Court of the Territory of Montana, 1865; Chief Justice for three years. Great-great-great-grandson of Thomas Hooker through Kesia Stiles Munson.

notable.notable_blurb: First Lincoln-appointed judge to Montana Territory, 1865

narrative_blocks.header: Lincoln sent him to judge the Montana frontier
narrative_blocks.body: In 1865 President Lincoln appointed Munson the first judge of the supreme court of Montana Territory, where he served as chief justice. He reached Helena that July and held the bench until resigning in 1868.

narrative_blocks.header: His mother was Edwin Stanton's cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Munson's mother was a cousin of Edwin Stanton, Lincoln's secretary of war, a tie that set him near the administration that sent him west. A Yale Law graduate of 1851, he had trained for the bench in New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: He came home to New Haven for good
narrative_blocks.body: After resigning the Montana bench in 1868, Munson settled back in New Haven where he had studied law. He died there in 1908 and lies in Grove Street Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: She outlived her husband by 4 years
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy A. Sanford of North Haven, Connecticut married Lyman Ezra Munson on October 6, 1846 — the same day noted in their circle as the date Lucy's sister married George Munson, Lyman's brother. Lyman died February 13, 1908.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST035.name: United States Military Academy (West Point)
INST035.notes: Founded 1802 at West Point NY. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Rev. George Washington Woodbridge (HD2163), son of General Jonathan Woodbridge (H00928), graduated West Point; served as 2nd Lieutenant US Army 1826-1829 before entering the Episcopal ministry. He became rector of Monumental Church Richmond VA 1843-1878 and escorted Flora Cooke Stuart to her dying husband Jeb Stuart in May 1864.

crossConnections.display_label: Both Hooker descendants in American broadcasting — Munson founding Armed Forces Radio; Hulbert Taft publishing the Cincinnati Times-Star.
